stem: MICR stands for
explanation: MICR (Magnetic Ink Character Reader) is kind of scanner that can scan and identify the writing of magnetic ink. This device is used in banks to verify signatures in Checks.

stem: EBCDIC stands for

options.label: A
options.text: Extended Binary Coded Decimal Interchange Code

options.label: B
options.text: Extended Bit Code Decimal Interchange Code

options.label: C
options.text: Extended Bit Case Decimal Interchange Code

options.label: D
options.text: Extended Binary Case Decimal Interchange Code

explanation: EBCDIC is an 8-bit binary code for larger IBMs primarily mainframes in which each byte represent one alphanumeric character or two decimal digits. 256 characters can be coded using EBCDIC.

stem: BCD is
explanation: BCD is a binary coded notation in which each of the decimal digits is expressed as a 8-bit binary numeral. For example in binary coded decimal notation 12 is 0001 0010 as opposed to 1100 in pure binary.

stem: ASCII stands for

options.label: A
options.text: American Stable Code for International Interchange

options.label: B
options.text: American Standard Case for Institutional Interchange

options.label: C
options.text: American Standard Code for Information Interchange

options.label: D
options.text: American Standard Code for Interchange Information

explanation: ASCII is a code which converts characters – letters, digits, punctuations and control characters such as Alt, Tab etc – into numeral form. ASCII code is used to represent data internally in micro-computers. ASCII codes are 7 bits and can represent 0 to 127 and extended ASCII are 8 bits that represents 0 to 255.

stem: Which of the following is first generation of computer
explanation: IBM-1401, CDC-1604 is second generation computer. ICL-2900 is a fourth generation computer. EDSAC is important in the development of computer since it was the first computer to use John von. Neumann’s Stored Program Concept. It used 3000 vacuum tubes and computers with vacuum tubes are of first generation computers.

stem: Chief component of first generation computer was
explanation: Transistors were used for second generation computers and integrated circuits in third generation. First generation computers used vacuum tubes and valves as their main electronic component. Vacuum Tubes were invented by Lee DeForest in 1908.

stem: FORTRAN is
explanation: FORTRAN (Formula Translation) is one of the earlier High Level programming languages used to write scientific applications. It was developed by IBM in 1956.

stem: EEPROM stand for

options.label: A
options.text: Electrically Erasable Programmable Read Only Memory

options.label: B
options.text: Easily Erasable Programmable Read Only Memory

options.label: C
options.text: Electronic Erasable Programmable Read Only Memory

explanation: There are three types of ROM namely, PROM, EPROM and EEPROM. PROM can’t be reprogrammed, EPROM can be erased by exposing it in high intensity ultraviolet light and EEPROM can be erased and reprogrammed electrically. It is not needed to be removed from the computer to be modified.

stem: Second Generation computers were developed during
explanation: Second generation computers used transistors as their main electronic component. Transistor was invented by Bell Lab Scientists John Burdeen, Walter Brattain and William Shockley in 1947 and won the Nobel Prize in 1956 but it was not used in computers till 1956. The second generation continued until the implementation of IC chips invented by Jack Kilby in Texas Instruments in 1958.

stem: The computer size was very large in
explanation: It is obvious that computers developed with more power, reliability, speed and smaller sizes due to the enhancement of technology. First generation computers used 1000s of vacuum tubes that required lot of space made them gigantic in size. Single transistor could replace 1000 vacuum tubes and a single IC chip replaced 1000s of transistors made computers smaller and more speedy.

stem: Microprocessors as switching devices are for which generation computers
explanation: Microprocessors further revolutionized the development of computers. Personal microcomputers were possible due to the microprocessors. The first microprocessor called Intel 4004 was developed by American Intel Corporation in 1971. Microprocessors are used in the computers of fourth generation computers.

stem: Which of the following devices can be sued to directly image printed text?
explanation: OCR (Optical Character Reader) scans printed or typed text and converts the shapes of the characters into machine-readable text, so it images printed text directly. OMR only detects the presence of pencil marks in fixed positions (bubble sheets), and MICR only reads characters printed in magnetic ink (cheque routing numbers), so neither handles general printed text and 'All of above' is wrong.

stem: The output quality of a printer is measured by
explanation: Answer unverified: the circulated answer key marks B ('dot per sq. inch'), but print resolution is actually specified in DPI = dots per linear inch (300, 600, 1200 dpi), which is option A, and the review panel resolved to A. Option C is the classic trap: dots printed per unit time is printing speed (pages per minute), not output quality, which also rules out 'All of above'. If the grader uses the printed key, expect B; if it uses the real definition, expect A.

stem: In analog computer

options.label: A
options.text: Input is first converted to digital form

options.label: B
options.text: Input is never converted to digital form

options.label: C
options.text: Output is displayed in digital form

explanation: An analog computer represents values as continuous physical quantities (voltage, shaft rotation, fluid pressure) and computes on them directly, so the input is never converted to digital form and the output stays analog too. Options A and C describe a digital or hybrid machine, which makes 'All of above' wrong as well.

stem: In latest generation computers, the instructions are executed
explanation: Modern CPUs still fetch instructions in program order but overlap them with pipelining, superscalar issue, and multiple cores, so execution is both sequential and parallel. Picking 'Parallel only' is the trap: the programmer's model and the retirement order remain sequential even when the hardware runs several instructions at once.

stem: Who designed the first electronics computer – ENIAC?

options.label: C
options.text: J. Presper Eckert and John W Mauchly

explanation: ENIAC was designed and built by J. Presper Eckert and John W. Mauchly at the University of Pennsylvania. Von Neumann is the classic distractor: he joined the project later and is associated with the stored-program architecture of EDVAC, not with the design of ENIAC itself.

stem: Who invented the high level language c?
explanation: C was created by Dennis M. Ritchie at Bell Labs in the early 1970s. The distractors are each famous for something else: Niklaus Wirth for Pascal, Seymour Papert for Logo, Donald Knuth for TeX and The Art of Computer Programming.

stem: Personnel who design, program, operate and maintain computer equipment refers to
explanation: Peopleware is the standard term for the human component of a computing system, sitting alongside hardware and software. Programmer, console operator, and system analyst are each only one specific role within peopleware, so they are too narrow for a definition that spans design, programming, operation, and maintenance.

stem: When did arch rivals IBM and Apple Computers Inc. decide to join hands?
explanation: IBM and Apple joined forces in 1991 in what became the AIM alliance (Apple, IBM, Motorola), which produced the PowerPC chip and the Taligent and Kaleida ventures. 1984 is the trap: that is the Macintosh launch year, not the alliance.

stem: Human beings are referred to as Homosapinens, which device is called Sillico Sapiens?
explanation: 'Silico Sapiens' is a coined nickname for the computer, playing on Homo sapiens by swapping carbon-based thinking for silicon-based. Pure terminology trivia with no reasoning behind it.

stem: An error in software or hardware is called a bug. What is the alternative computer jargon for it?
explanation: Glitch is the alternative jargon for a bug, that is a fault in hardware or software. Leech, squid, and slug are not computing fault terms and exist here only as filler distractors.

stem: Modern Computer are very reliable but they are not
explanation: The stock phrasing is 'computers are reliable but not infallible': they execute correctly, but they still produce wrong results from wrong input or wrong programs (garbage in, garbage out). Fast, powerful, and cheap all describe what modern computers are, so only 'infallible' completes a negation.

stem: What is the name of the display feature that highlights are of the screen which requires operator attention?
explanation: Reverse video swaps the foreground and background colours of a screen region so it stands out and draws the operator's attention. A pixel is just the smallest addressable dot, a cursor only marks the input position, and a touch screen is an input technology, so none of them is a highlighting feature.

stem: IMB launched its first personal computer called IBM-PC in 1981. It had chips from Intel, disk drives from Tandon, operating system from Microsoft, the printer from Epson and the application software from everywhere. Can you name the country which contributed the video display?
explanation: The video display for the original 1981 IBM-PC was supplied from Taiwan. Legacy trivia with no reasoning behind it; memorize the pairing (Intel chips, Tandon drives, Microsoft OS, Epson printer, Taiwan display).

stem: Personal computers use a number of chips mounted on a main circuit board. What is the common name for such boards?
explanation: The main circuit board that carries the chips in a PC is the motherboard. A daughter board is a smaller board that plugs into the motherboard rather than being the main board, and a breadboard is a solderless prototyping board, not a production PC board.
